emergent_themes.description: Takeaway as a practical household solution when family logistics break down - both parents working, busy schedules, empty fridge
Family awareness of takeaway frequency but reluctance to acknowledge it as dependency - 'unspoken ritual' language
Social context providing moral permission for choices that feel problematic when done alone - friends make takeaway feel 'not as guilty'
Bad delivery experiences creating lasting preference for direct ordering over aggregator platforms due to accountability issues
Fear that subscriptions will increase ordering frequency beyond comfortable levels - 'if I have one, I'll try to make the most of it'
Platform recognition issues when brand names don't clearly communicate food delivery purpose - Deliveroo confused with courier service

coding_summary: Lee demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of their family's food ecosystem, where takeaway serves as a practical necessity rather than indulgence. Key patterns include: (1) Family logistics driving delivery decisions when both parents work full-time, (2) Social context legitimizing choices that feel guilty when done alone, (3) Friday takeaway as an 'unspoken ritual' the family won't acknowledge as dependency, (4) Bad aggregator experience creating lasting trust issues, (5) Subscription avoidance due to fear of increased ordering frequency, (6) Strong price consciousness and deal-seeking behavior, (7) Brand confusion around Deliveroo's purpose. Lee shows clear post-graduation aspirations for better eating habits but realistic expectations about continued takeaway use.

coverage_gaps.reason: Lee lives with parents, so no data on independent accommodation impact on eating routines

spk_1: I think, as a student, just subscriptions as a whole are something I'd avoid because it's like...
